text:
Gross domestic product has long served as the primary standard for measuring a nation's economic performance, but a growing consensus suggests that this single measure fails to capture the full picture of social well-being. In response, the United Nations is now considering a set of 31 new measures designed to complement and extend beyond GDP. These proposed yearly measures include not only economic data, such as household income left after spending per person, but also natural factors like heat-trapping gas releases and air quality. Additionally, they include health and education measures, including life length and students' school performance, as well as social measures such as how common violence against women and girls is.

While efforts to challenge the leading role of GDP date back at least three decades, this effort marks the first time a UN chief has formally responded to a request from member states on this matter. The report, put together by a team of experts from many fields and decision-makers, was described by the UN Secretary-General as a major step in correcting a long-standing blind spot in how progress is measured. He noted that the global economy has expanded by over 50 percent during his time in office, yet many measures for health, biodiversity, job creation, and peace have moved in the opposite direction, with conflicts now at levels unseen since World War II.

GDP, according to one widely used method, is calculated as the total of what households and governments spend plus what businesses invest. Its rules are agreed upon by the UN Statistical Commission, though data are gathered and reported by individual countries. Despite its widespread use in decision-making and as a standard for defense, health, and research spending, critics argue that GDP was never intended for the purposes it now serves. The UN General Assembly president pointed out a basic contradiction: GDP can actually increase in the presence of harm, such as when road accidents or chemical disasters require costly countermeasures, thereby increasing economic output.

The proposed new measures have drawn mixed reactions from researchers and decision-makers. Some experts describe GDP as a deeply flawed measure of economic growth, arguing that well-being, inclusion, and sustainability represent better measures of social progress. Others warn that while these new measures offer a more complete view, their implementation and global adoption will require significant coordination and political will. The report represents a deliberate shift toward a more complete understanding of national prosperity, one that acknowledges that economic expansion alone does not necessarily mean improved quality of life for citizens.
